enrichment.short_description: Replicate is a cloud platform for running and fine-tuning open-source machine learning models via a simple API, billed per second of compute used.
enrichment.starting_price: From $0.000025 per second of CPU compute (GPU pricing from $0.000225/sec for Nvidia T4)
enrichment.seo_title: Replicate Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Replicate costs in 2026, how its per-second GPU pricing works, and whether this open-source AI model hosting platform fits your project.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Replicate is a cloud platform, founded in 2019 by Ben Firshman and Andreas Jansson, that lets developers run, fine-tune, and deploy thousands of open-source machine learning models through a single REST API without managing GPU infrastructure themselves. The company is headquartered in San Francisco, California, grew through Y Combinator, and raised roughly $57.8 million in venture funding from investors including Andreessen Horowitz and Sequoia Capital before reaching about 36 employees. In November 2025, Cloudflare announced it would acquire Replicate to fold its library of more than 50,000 AI models into the Cloudflare Workers AI ecosystem, with the deal closing on December 1, 2025; as of mid-2026 Replicate continues to operate under its own brand and pricing with no publicly announced Cloudflare-specific tiers. Replicate's core value proposition is removing the operational burden of serving machine learning models: users can call a hosted version of an open model (image generation, video generation, speech, upscaling, language models, and more) with a few lines of code, or package and push their own model to the platform using Cog, Replicate's open-source tool for containerizing ML models. Billing is strictly usage-based with no monthly subscription — customers pay for the exact hardware-seconds their predictions consume, with rates ranging from about $0.000025 per second for small CPU instances up to $0.001525 per second for a single Nvidia H100 GPU (roughly $5.49/hour), and multi-GPU configurations scaling up from there. For public community models, only active processing time is billed; for private models and always-on deployments, customers pay for setup and idle time as well as active compute. Some popular models, particularly large language models and generative image/video tools, use alternative output-based pricing measured in tokens, images, or seconds of generated media rather than raw compute time. Replicate also offers custom enterprise arrangements with volume discounts and committed-spend contracts for teams needing dedicated multi-GPU capacity. The platform is popular among startups, indie developers, and AI product teams who want to experiment with or ship state-of-the-art open models (such as Stable Diffusion variants, Flux, Whisper, and Llama-family models) quickly, without provisioning their own GPU servers or negotiating with cloud infrastructure providers. Because pricing is transparent and consumption-based, Replicate is often used for prototyping and variable-traffic production workloads where reserving dedicated GPU capacity would be wasteful.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Replicate is an AI infrastructure company that hosts thousands of open-source machine learning models and exposes them through a unified API, so developers can generate images, video, speech, and text without setting up their own GPU servers. Founded in 2019 by Ben Firshman and Andreas Jansson and based in San Francisco, the company built its reputation on Cog, an open-source packaging tool that lets any model be containerized and deployed to Replicate's infrastructure with predictable versioning.

In November 2025, Cloudflare announced an agreement to acquire Replicate in order to integrate its catalog of 50,000+ AI models into the Cloudflare Workers AI platform; the acquisition closed in December 2025. Replicate continues to run as its own product and brand within Cloudflare as of mid-2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Replicate's core feature is one-line API access to a large catalog of community and proprietary open-source models spanning image generation, video generation, audio/speech, upscaling, and large language models, with each model versioned so outputs stay reproducible over time. Developers who want to deploy their own custom model can use Cog to package code and dependencies into a standard container that Replicate can serve automatically on the appropriate GPU hardware.

Additional platform capabilities include webhooks for asynchronous predictions, fine-tuning support for select model families, private deployments with dedicated hardware, and an official Python and Node.js SDK for integrating predictions directly into application code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Replicate has no monthly subscription; every account pays strictly for the compute it consumes, billed per second of hardware time. CPU instances start at roughly $0.000025/sec, while GPU rates range from about $0.000225/sec for an Nvidia T4 up to $0.001525/sec for a single Nvidia H100, with multi-GPU configurations priced proportionally higher.

Public community models bill only for active prediction time, while private models and standing deployments also bill for idle and setup time since the hardware stays reserved. Certain high-traffic model categories, especially large language models and generative media models, instead bill per token or per second/frame of output. Organizations with large or predictable workloads can negotiate custom enterprise pricing with volume discounts and committed-spend contracts.

features.title: Model library API
features.description: Access thousands of pre-hosted open-source models for image, video, audio, and text generation through a single consistent REST API.

features.title: Cog packaging
features.description: Open-source tool for containerizing custom ML models so they run reliably on Replicate's infrastructure with reproducible versioning.

features.title: Per-second usage billing
features.description: No subscription fees — pay only for the CPU or GPU seconds a prediction actually consumes.

features.title: Custom model deployment
features.description: Push and host your own fine-tuned or proprietary models alongside community models.

features.title: Fine-tuning support
features.description: Train and fine-tune select model families (e.g., image and language models) directly on the platform.

features.title: Webhooks and async predictions
features.description: Long-running predictions can notify your application via webhook instead of blocking on a synchronous API call.

features.title: Private deployments
features.description: Reserve dedicated hardware for a specific model to guarantee latency and availability for production workloads.

features.title: Multi-GPU configurations
features.description: Scale predictions across multiple H100 or A100 GPUs for large or latency-sensitive workloads.

pros: No subscription required — pay only for actual compute used
Very large and constantly updated catalog of open-source models
Cog makes packaging and deploying custom models straightforward
Transparent, publicly listed per-second GPU pricing
Backed by Cloudflare's infrastructure following the 2025 acquisition
cons: Costs can scale unpredictably with heavy or bursty usage since there is no flat plan
Private models and deployments bill for idle time, not just active processing
Cold starts can add latency for infrequently used models
Enterprise-scale committed-spend pricing is not publicly listed and requires a sales conversation
Some advanced model categories use less-transparent token or output-based pricing

faqs.question: What is Replicate used for?
faqs.answer: Replicate lets developers run and deploy open-source machine learning models, such as image generators, video generators, speech models, and large language models, through a simple API without managing their own GPU servers.

faqs.question: How does Replicate pricing work?
faqs.answer: Replicate has no monthly subscription. Customers pay per second of compute used, with rates starting around $0.000025/sec for CPU and going up to about $0.001525/sec for a single Nvidia H100 GPU; some models bill per token or per output unit instead.

faqs.question: Who founded Replicate and when?
faqs.answer: Replicate was founded in 2019 by Ben Firshman and Andreas Jansson, and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Is Replicate still an independent company?
faqs.answer: No. Cloudflare announced its acquisition of Replicate in November 2025, and the deal closed in December 2025. Replicate continues to operate under its own brand within Cloudflare.

faqs.question: What is Cog?
faqs.answer: Cog is Replicate's open-source tool for packaging machine learning models and their dependencies into standard containers that can be deployed and versioned on Replicate's infrastructure.

faqs.question: Does Replicate offer a free tier?
faqs.answer: Replicate does not advertise a dedicated free subscription tier; new accounts typically pay as they go for compute used, and enterprise customers can negotiate committed-spend contracts.

faqs.question: Can I deploy my own custom model on Replicate?
faqs.answer: Yes. Developers can package a custom or fine-tuned model with Cog and push it to Replicate, where it can be served through the same API used for community models.

faqs.question: Does Replicate support fine-tuning?
faqs.answer: Yes, Replicate supports fine-tuning for select model families, allowing users to train models on their own data before deploying them.

use_cases.title: AI product prototyping
use_cases.description: Startups and indie developers use Replicate to test generative AI features like image or video generation in a product without provisioning GPU infrastructure.
use_cases.audience: Startup engineering teams and indie developers

use_cases.title: Variable-traffic production inference
use_cases.description: Teams with unpredictable or bursty inference traffic use Replicate's per-second billing to avoid paying for idle reserved GPU capacity.
use_cases.audience: AI product teams with fluctuating usage

use_cases.title: Custom model hosting
use_cases.description: Teams that fine-tune their own models use Cog to package and deploy them on Replicate's managed hardware for API access.

features: Billed only for active processing time on public models

enrichment.short_description: Replit is a browser-based coding and AI app-building platform that lets developers write, run, deploy, and collaborate on software from any device, powered by its AI coding agent.
enrichment.pricing_model: freemium with usage credits (subscription plus consumption)
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Starter plan); Core plan from $20/month billed annually
enrichment.seo_title: Replit Review 2026: Pricing, AI Agent, and Features Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: A 2026 look at Replit's pricing tiers, AI Agent capabilities, and how the browser-based coding platform compares for building and deploying apps.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Replit is a cloud-based software development platform, founded in 2016 by Amjad Masad, Haya Odeh, and Faris Masad, that lets anyone write, run, and deploy code entirely in the browser, and has increasingly repositioned itself around an AI coding agent that can build and ship full applications from natural-language prompts. The company is headquartered at 1001 East Hillsdale Boulevard in Foster City, California, having relocated there from San Francisco in 2024, and reports several hundred employees. Replit has raised roughly $922 million in total funding from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Craft Ventures, and Reach Capital; in March 2026 it closed a $400 million Series D led by Georgian at a $9 billion valuation, roughly triple its valuation six months earlier, and the company has stated it is targeting $1 billion in annualized run-rate revenue by the end of 2026. Replit's product spans a cloud IDE with instant environments for dozens of languages, real-time multiplayer collaboration, a built-in database and hosting layer for full-stack apps, and Replit Agent, its AI system that can scaffold, write, debug, and deploy software from a plain-language description. As of 2026 Replit offers four tiers: a free Starter plan with limited daily Agent credits and one publishable app; Core at $20/month billed annually (or $25 month-to-month) with $25 in monthly usage credits, up to five collaborators, and the ability to run two AI agents in parallel; Pro, which replaced the former Teams plan in February 2026, at $95/month billed annually (or $100 month-to-month) with $100 in monthly credits, up to 15 collaborators, 50 viewers, ten parallel agents, access to the most capable AI models, and 28-day database rollbacks; and a custom-priced Enterprise plan with SSO/SAML, single-tenant environments, VPC peering, and dedicated support for regulated industries. Because usage credits rather than flat subscription fees drive most of the real cost, actual monthly bills can exceed the advertised plan price once AI Agent usage surpasses the included credit allotment. Replit is used by students learning to code, hobbyists building side projects, and increasingly by non-technical founders and enterprise teams using the Agent to generate production-ready web and mobile applications without writing code by hand; the company has said users from roughly 85% of the Fortune 500 have built something on the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Replit began in 2016 as a browser-based IDE that let developers code, run, and share projects instantly without local setup, and has since evolved into an AI-first app-building platform centered on Replit Agent, which can generate, debug, and deploy full applications from natural-language instructions. Co-founded by Amjad Masad, Haya Odeh, and Faris Masad, the company is now headquartered in Foster City, California.

Fueled by a $400 million Series D in March 2026 at a $9 billion valuation, Replit has grown rapidly on the back of AI-assisted software creation, positioning itself for both individual developers and enterprise teams building internal tools and customer-facing apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Replit's core is a cloud development environment accessible from any browser, supporting dozens of programming languages with zero local setup, real-time multiplayer editing, and one-click hosting and deployment for web apps. Its most differentiated feature is Replit Agent, an AI system capable of planning, writing, testing, and iterating on full applications, including provisioning a built-in Postgres-backed database, from a natural-language prompt.

Collaboration tools include shared workspaces with configurable collaborator and viewer limits, deployment privacy controls (public, private, or password-protected), and database rollback features on higher tiers for recovering from AI-driven mistakes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Replit offers four tiers. Starter is free and includes limited daily Agent credits, a built-in database, and the ability to publish one project. Core costs $20/month billed annually (or $25/month billed monthly) and adds $25 in monthly usage credits, up to five collaborators, two parallel AI agents, unlimited workspaces, and removal of the 'Made with Replit' badge.

Pro, introduced in February 2026 to replace the former Teams plan, costs $95/month billed annually (or $100/month billed monthly) and includes $100 in monthly credits, up to 15 collaborators and 50 viewers, ten parallel agents, access to the most capable AI models, and 28-day database rollbacks. Enterprise pricing is custom and adds SSO/SAML, single-tenant environments, VPC peering, and dedicated support; Replit has removed public enterprise pricing from its website in favor of a sales-led process.

features.title: Replit Agent
features.description: AI system that plans, writes, tests, and deploys full applications from natural-language prompts, including database and backend provisioning.

features.title: Cloud IDE
features.description: Browser-based development environment supporting dozens of languages with no local installation required.

features.title: Real-time multiplayer collaboration
features.description: Multiple users can edit and run the same project simultaneously, similar to collaborative document editing.

features.title: Built-in database and hosting
features.description: Full-stack apps get an integrated database and one-click deployment without separate infrastructure setup.

features.title: Parallel AI agents
features.description: Higher-tier plans allow running multiple Agent sessions in parallel to build or modify different parts of a project simultaneously.

features.title: Deployment privacy controls
features.description: Publish apps as public, private, or password-protected, with custom domains available on paid plans.

features.title: Database rollbacks
features.description: Pro plan supports rolling back the database up to 28 days, useful for recovering from AI-driven changes.

features.title: Enterprise security controls
features.description: SSO/SAML, single-tenant environments, and VPC peering available on the custom Enterprise plan.

pros: AI Agent can generate and deploy working applications from plain-language prompts
No local setup required — fully browser-based development and hosting
Real-time collaboration makes it useful for pair programming and teaching
Built-in database and deployment remove the need for separate infrastructure
Rapid growth and heavy funding suggest strong ongoing product investment
cons: Usage-credit model means real costs can exceed the advertised subscription price
Enterprise pricing is no longer public and requires a sales conversation
AI-generated code and database changes can require careful review before production use
Parallel agent and collaborator limits are capped by plan tier
Frequent pricing and plan restructuring (e.g., Teams retirement) can make cost planning harder

faqs.question: What is Replit used for?
faqs.answer: Replit is a browser-based platform for writing, running, and deploying code, and increasingly for building full applications using its AI coding agent, Replit Agent, from natural-language prompts.

faqs.question: How much does Replit cost?
faqs.answer: Replit has a free Starter plan, a Core plan at $20/month billed annually ($25 monthly), a Pro plan at $95/month billed annually ($100 monthly), and a custom-priced Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: What happened to Replit's Teams plan?
faqs.answer: Replit retired its Teams plan in February 2026 and replaced it with a new Pro plan that includes more collaborators, parallel agents, and access to more capable AI models.

faqs.question: Who founded Replit?
faqs.answer: Replit was founded in 2016 by Amjad Masad, Haya Odeh, and Faris Masad.

faqs.question: Where is Replit headquartered?
faqs.answer: Replit is headquartered at 1001 East Hillsdale Boulevard in Foster City, California, after relocating from San Francisco in 2024.

faqs.question: What is Replit Agent?
faqs.answer: Replit Agent is Replit's AI system that can plan, write, test, and deploy software, including provisioning databases and backend logic, from a natural-language description.

faqs.question: How much funding has Replit raised?
faqs.answer: Replit has raised approximately $922 million in total funding, including a $400 million Series D in March 2026 at a $9 billion valuation.

faqs.question: Do usage credits expire on Replit?
faqs.answer: Yes, monthly usage credits included with Core and Pro plans generally expire if unused within a set period, so actual usage patterns affect total cost.

use_cases.title: AI-assisted app building
use_cases.description: Non-technical founders and product teams use Replit Agent to generate and deploy working web applications from natural-language prompts.

use_cases.title: Learning to code
use_cases.description: Students and hobbyists use Replit's free, zero-setup browser IDE to write and run code in dozens of languages without installing anything locally.

use_cases.title: Collaborative development and prototyping
use_cases.description: Engineering teams use Replit's real-time multiplayer editing and instant deployment to prototype and pair-program on projects together.

features: Access to the most powerful AI models
Database rollbacks for up to 28 days

enrichment.short_description: Repurpose.io is an automated content repurposing and distribution platform that resizes and republishes video and audio content across TikTok, Instagram, YouTube Shorts, Facebook, and other social platforms.
enrichment.seo_title: Repurpose.io Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Automation
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Repurpose.io's pricing, plans, and automated workflows help creators repurpose video and podcast content across social platforms in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Repurpose.io is a content automation platform founded in November 2016 by CEO Hani Mourra, who previously built SimpleSocialPress.com, and is headquartered in Austin, Texas, operating with a lean team of roughly 9 to 11 employees. The platform's core function is 'fire-and-forget' content repurposing: it automatically pulls new video, Reels, Shorts, Stories, or podcast episodes from a source platform, resizes and reformats them for each destination, removes watermarks, and publishes them across more than 15 channels including TikTok, YouTube Shorts, Instagram, Facebook, Pinterest, LinkedIn, X, Amazon, BlueSky, and Twitch, without requiring the creator to manually re-upload or reformat anything after the initial workflow is set up. The company markets itself as an official platform partner of Meta, YouTube, TikTok, Snapchat, and Amazon, arguing that routing automation through approved API integrations avoids the account-flagging risk associated with unofficial scraping or browser-automation tools, and highlights a decade-long partnership history along with a Stories-repurposing capability it says is not matched by direct competitors. As of 2026, Repurpose.io is priced on three monthly tiers: Starter at $35/month (or $349/year on annual billing, roughly a 17% discount) supporting up to 3 connected accounts per social network and 5,000 published videos per month, aimed at solo creators and small business owners; Pro at $79/month ($790/year) raising the account limit to 10 per network with unlimited published videos, aimed at growing teams and creators managing multiple brands; and Agency at $179/month ($1,790/year) supporting up to 25 accounts per network with unlimited publishing, aimed at agencies managing multiple clients. All plans include a 14-day free trial, and new users can test the platform with 10 free video publishes before subscribing. Repurpose.io differentiates itself from general social media schedulers by focusing specifically on automated cross-platform redistribution of existing long-form or short-form content rather than original content creation or generic post scheduling, making it popular with podcasters, YouTubers, TikTok creators, and course creators who want to multiply the reach of content they have already produced without manually re-editing and re-uploading it to every platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Repurpose.io was founded in November 2016 by Hani Mourra and is based in Austin, Texas. The platform automates the process of taking a single piece of video, Reel, Short, Story, or podcast content and republishing it, correctly resized and formatted, across more than 15 social and content platforms without manual re-uploading.

The company positions itself as an official platform partner of Meta, YouTube, TikTok, Snapchat, and Amazon, which it says allows its automation to run through approved APIs rather than risky workarounds, reducing the chance of accounts being flagged or automation breaking when platforms update their systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Repurpose.io's central feature is workflow-based automation: a creator connects a source account (for example TikTok or a podcast RSS feed) and one or more destination accounts, and every new piece of content is automatically pulled, reformatted for each destination's aspect ratio and requirements, watermark-removed where applicable, and published on a schedule or in near real time.

The platform supports distribution to TikTok, YouTube Shorts, Instagram (including Reels and Stories), Facebook, Pinterest, LinkedIn, X, Amazon, BlueSky, and Twitch, and includes tools for converting podcast audio into short video clips for social distribution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Repurpose.io offers three monthly subscription tiers, each with a discounted annual option. Starter is $35/month ($349/year) with up to 3 connected accounts per network and 5,000 published videos monthly; Pro is $79/month ($790/year) with up to 10 accounts per network and unlimited published videos; and Agency is $179/month ($1,790/year) with up to 25 accounts per network and unlimited publishing.

Annual billing saves roughly 17% compared to paying monthly. All plans include a 14-day free trial, and prospective users can try the platform with 10 free video publishes before committing to a paid subscription.

faqs.answer: Repurpose.io automatically pulls video, Reels, Shorts, Stories, or podcast content from a source platform and republishes it, resized and reformatted, across 15+ destination platforms like TikTok, Instagram, YouTube Shorts, and Facebook.

faqs.question: How much does Repurpose.io cost?
faqs.answer: Repurpose.io has three plans: Starter at $35/month, Pro at $79/month, and Agency at $179/month, with roughly 17% savings available on annual billing.

faqs.question: Who founded Repurpose.io?
faqs.answer: Repurpose.io was founded in November 2016 by Hani Mourra and is based in Austin, Texas.

faqs.question: Does Repurpose.io offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, all plans include a 14-day free trial, and new users can also try the platform with 10 free video publishes before subscribing.

faqs.question: Which platforms does Repurpose.io support?
faqs.answer: Repurpose.io supports TikTok, YouTube Shorts, Instagram, Facebook, Pinterest, LinkedIn, X, Amazon, BlueSky, and Twitch, among other destinations.

faqs.question: Is Repurpose.io an official partner of social platforms?
faqs.answer: Yes, the company states it is an official platform partner of Meta, YouTube, TikTok, Snapchat, and Amazon.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the Starter, Pro, and Agency plans?
faqs.answer: The plans differ mainly in the number of connected accounts per social network (3, 10, or 25) and monthly video publishing limits (5,000 on Starter versus unlimited on Pro and Agency).

faqs.question: Can Repurpose.io remove TikTok watermarks?
faqs.answer: Yes, the platform automatically removes source-platform watermarks, such as TikTok's, before republishing content to other channels.

enrichment.short_description: Reputation (Reputation.com) is an enterprise reputation and experience management platform that helps multi-location businesses monitor reviews, surveys, and search/AI visibility across locations.
enrichment.starting_price: From approximately $80/month entry point, though most deployments require custom enterprise quotes
enrichment.seo_title: Reputation.com Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Enterprise Use Cases
enrichment.seo_meta_description: An overview of Reputation.com's enterprise reputation management platform, pricing structure, and key features for multi-location businesses in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Reputation, commonly known as Reputation.com, is an enterprise reputation and customer experience management platform founded in 2006 by Michael Fertik and Brian Kelley. The company originally operated as ReputationDefender, focused on consumer online-reputation services, before pivoting toward enterprise B2B reputation management around 2011. It is headquartered at 6111 Bollinger Canyon Road, Suite 500, San Ramon, California, and employs roughly 693 people as of 2026. The company has raised approximately $325.3 million in total funding across multiple rounds from investors including Kleiner Perkins, Bessemer Venture Partners, and Marlin Equity Partners, with its most recent disclosed valuation at approximately $730 million post-money following a $150 million minority investment led by Marlin Equity Partners in January 2022, advised by Goldman Sachs. Reputation.com markets itself as an AI-native reputation management platform built to help multi-location businesses be found, trusted, and chosen across traditional search engines and newer AI-driven answer engines, and its ideal customer profile centers on enterprise organizations with 50 to 500 employees, $10 million or more in annual recurring revenue, and 10 or more physical locations, spanning industries like healthcare systems, automotive dealership groups, property management firms, restaurant chains, and retail networks. The core platform aggregates and analyzes online reviews, survey feedback, social listening, and local business listing data across every location a brand operates, and provides tools for responding to reviews at scale, benchmarking location-level reputation against competitors, and increasingly, tracking how a brand appears within AI-generated search and chatbot answers. Pricing is not publicly tiered; while some sources cite an entry point around $80 per month for a Core-level offering, real-world enterprise deployments for organizations managing dozens or hundreds of locations typically range from several thousand to over $20,000 per month depending on the number of locations and brands managed, the specific modules licensed (reviews, surveys, social, listings, AI visibility), data volume, level of customization, number of user seats, and contract length. There is no publicly available free trial; prospective customers must go through a sales-led demo and custom quote process. Reputation.com is used primarily by multi-location enterprises — such as auto dealer groups, healthcare systems, and multi-unit retail or restaurant brands — that need centralized visibility and control over their online reputation and customer feedback across dozens or hundreds of individual locations, rather than by small businesses or single-location operators.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Reputation was founded in 2006 by Michael Fertik and Brian Kelley, originally as ReputationDefender, a consumer-focused online reputation service, before shifting to an enterprise B2B model around 2011. The company is now headquartered in San Ramon, California, and markets itself as an AI-native reputation management platform for multi-location businesses.

Reputation.com has raised roughly $325.3 million in funding over its history, with its most recent disclosed valuation around $730 million following a $150 million investment led by Marlin Equity Partners in January 2022. The company reports around 693 employees as of 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform centralizes review monitoring and response, customer survey collection, social listening, and local business listing management across every location a brand operates, giving multi-location businesses a single dashboard to track reputation at scale. It increasingly includes tools focused on how a brand is represented in AI-generated search and chatbot answers, alongside traditional search-engine visibility.

Reputation.com is designed for enterprise customers with complex organizational structures, offering location-level benchmarking, competitive analysis, and role-based access so franchise or multi-brand organizations can manage reputation consistently across dozens or hundreds of locations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Reputation.com does not publish standard tiered pricing; the platform is sold through a custom enterprise sales process, with quotes based on the number of locations and brands managed, which modules are licensed, data volume, customization needs, user seat count, and contract length.

While some third-party sources cite an entry-level price point around $80/month, most enterprise deployments — particularly for organizations with 10 or more locations — range from several thousand to upwards of $20,000 per month. There is no publicly advertised free trial; prospective customers work with the sales team to scope and price a deployment.

features.title: Multi-location review monitoring
features.description: Aggregates and tracks online reviews across every business location from a single centralized dashboard.

features.title: Review response management
features.description: Tools to respond to customer reviews at scale, including templated and AI-assisted responses.

features.title: Customer survey collection
features.description: Built-in survey tools to gather structured customer feedback alongside organic review data.

features.title: Social listening
features.description: Monitors social media mentions and sentiment relevant to the brand and its individual locations.

features.title: Business listings management
features.description: Keeps local business listings (name, address, phone, hours) accurate and consistent across directories.

features.title: Location-level benchmarking
features.description: Compares reputation metrics across a brand's own locations and against competitors.

features.title: AI search visibility tracking
features.description: Monitors how a brand is represented within AI-generated search and chatbot answers, not just traditional search results.

features.title: Role-based enterprise access

pros: Purpose-built for large multi-location enterprises with complex reputation needs
Combines reviews, surveys, social listening, and listings management in one platform
Increasingly tracks AI-driven search and chatbot visibility, not just traditional SEO
Long operating history (founded 2006) with substantial enterprise customer base
Backed by significant funding and private equity investment, suggesting platform stability
cons: No public pricing — requires a sales-led custom quote process
No free trial available for prospective customers
Enterprise-oriented pricing (potentially thousands of dollars per month) may be out of reach for small businesses
Best suited to organizations with 10+ locations, limiting fit for single-location businesses
Complex feature set may require onboarding investment to use effectively

faqs.question: What does Reputation.com do?
faqs.answer: Reputation.com is an enterprise reputation and customer experience management platform that helps multi-location businesses monitor and manage online reviews, surveys, social listening, and search/AI visibility across all their locations.

faqs.question: How much does Reputation.com cost?
faqs.answer: Reputation.com does not publish standard pricing. While some sources cite an entry point around $80/month, most enterprise deployments cost from several thousand to over $20,000 per month depending on locations, modules, and contract terms.

faqs.question: Who founded Reputation.com and when?
faqs.answer: Reputation.com was founded in 2006 by Michael Fertik and Brian Kelley, originally as ReputationDefender, before pivoting to an enterprise B2B model around 2011.

faqs.question: Where is Reputation.com headquartered?
faqs.answer: Reputation.com is headquartered in San Ramon, California.

faqs.question: Does Reputation.com offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: No, Reputation.com does not offer a publicly available free trial; pricing and access are handled through a custom sales process.

faqs.question: Who is Reputation.com designed for?
faqs.answer: Reputation.com primarily serves enterprise organizations with 10 or more locations, such as healthcare systems, automotive dealership groups, restaurant chains, and retail networks.

faqs.question: Does Reputation.com help with AI search visibility?
faqs.answer: Yes, the platform has expanded to help brands monitor and improve how they appear in AI-generated search and chatbot answers, in addition to traditional search engine results.

faqs.question: How much funding has Reputation.com raised?
faqs.answer: Reputation.com has raised approximately $325.3 million in total funding, with a $150 million investment from Marlin Equity Partners in January 2022 valuing the company at roughly $730 million.

use_cases.title: Multi-location review management
use_cases.description: Auto dealer groups and retail chains use Reputation.com to monitor and respond to reviews across dozens or hundreds of individual locations from one dashboard.

use_cases.title: Healthcare system reputation monitoring
use_cases.description: Healthcare systems use the platform to track patient feedback and reviews across many facilities and providers while maintaining compliance-conscious response workflows.

use_cases.title: Franchise brand consistency
use_cases.description: Franchise organizations use location-level benchmarking and role-based access to keep reputation management consistent across independently operated franchise locations.

enrichment.short_description: Request Tracker (RT) is an open-source ticketing and workflow management system built by Best Practical Solutions, used by IT, support, and operations teams to track tickets, assets, and SLAs.
enrichment.pricing_model: open source (self-hosted, free) with paid Cloud RT and support/services options
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted, open source); hosted Cloud RT and support plans priced separately by Best Practical Solutions
languages: and 35+ interface languages via community translations
enrichment.seo_title: Request Tracker (RT) Review 2026: Open-Source Ticketing Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn how Request Tracker (RT), Best Practical Solutions' open-source ticketing system, works, what it costs to self-host or run in the cloud, and its key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Request Tracker, widely known as RT, is an open-source ticketing and workflow management system originally released in 1996 by Jesse Vincent, who began developing it as a student at Wesleyan University working on the school's computing help desk, before founding Best Practical Solutions LLC to develop, distribute, and support the software commercially. Best Practical Solutions is a small company headquartered in Somerville, Massachusetts, with roughly 9 to 11 employees, and reported revenue in the range of about $12 million. RT is distributed under the GNU General Public License and remains free and open source to self-host, with source code maintained publicly on GitHub; Best Practical also offers a hosted Cloud RT service and paid training, consulting, and support packages for organizations that prefer not to self-manage the software. The platform is designed for IT help desks, customer support teams, and internal operations groups that need to track and route incoming requests as structured tickets, and after nearly three decades of development it includes email integration for creating and updating tickets by email, a visual Lifecycle builder for customizing ticket workflows and states, an automation engine called Scrips and Actions for triggering rules-based responses, a built-in knowledge base (Articles) for FAQs and canned responses, asset management for tracking associated equipment or configuration items, SLA management with business-hours awareness, role-based security and granular permissions, a REST v2 API, Zapier integration, and a multi-language interface supporting more than 35 languages. RT also has a specialized variant, RT for Incident Response (RTIR), aimed at security teams handling incident tickets. Because RT is free to download and self-host under an open-source license, third-party pricing aggregators often cite tiered per-user monthly pricing (for example, figures around $25 to $105 per user per month across Starter, Business, Growth, and Enterprise tiers) that reflect Best Practical's hosted Cloud RT and support offerings rather than a cost required to use the core open-source software itself. RT is popular in academic institutions, government agencies, and IT organizations that value self-hosting, data control, and the absence of vendor lock-in over the convenience of a fully managed SaaS helpdesk, and it has more than 400 freely available community-built extensions that expand its functionality beyond the base install.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Request Tracker (RT) is one of the longest-running open-source ticketing systems in active use, first released in 1996 by Jesse Vincent and now developed and supported by Best Practical Solutions LLC, a small company based in Somerville, Massachusetts. RT is distributed under the GNU General Public License, meaning organizations can self-host it for free and modify the source code, which is maintained publicly on GitHub.

Best Practical Solutions also offers a hosted Cloud RT service along with training, consulting, and support packages for organizations that want RT's flexibility without managing their own servers. A specialized edition, RT for Incident Response (RTIR), targets security teams handling incident-response workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: RT provides email-based ticket creation and updates, a visual Lifecycle builder for customizing ticket states and workflows, an automation engine (Scrips and Actions) for rules-based ticket handling, a knowledge base for FAQs and canned responses, asset management, SLA tracking with business-hours support, and role-based permissions.

Integration options include a REST v2 API and Zapier connectivity, and the platform supports a multi-language interface covering more than 35 languages. More than 400 community-built extensions are freely available to extend RT's core functionality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Request Tracker software is free and open source to download and self-host under the GNU General Public License, with no licensing fee required. Organizations that prefer a managed option can subscribe to Best Practical's hosted Cloud RT service or purchase support, training, and consulting packages.

Third-party pricing trackers cite tiered monthly per-user pricing for RT-related services (roughly $25 to $105 per user/month across Starter, Business, Growth, and Enterprise tiers), which reflects Best Practical's Cloud RT hosting and support offerings rather than a fee for the open-source software itself.

features.title: Email-integrated ticketing

features.title: Visual Lifecycle builder
features.description: Customize ticket states and workflows visually to match an organization's internal processes.

features.title: Scrips and Actions automation
features.description: Rules-based automation engine that triggers actions or notifications based on ticket events.

features.title: Built-in knowledge base
features.description: Articles feature for storing FAQs and canned responses accessible to agents and, optionally, requesters.

features.title: Asset management
features.description: Track equipment and configuration items associated with tickets for IT operations use cases.

features.title: SLA management
features.description: Business-hours-aware SLA tracking to monitor response and resolution time commitments.

features.title: REST v2 API and Zapier integration
features.description: Programmatic access and no-code automation connections to other tools.

features.title: Role-based security
features.description: Granular permissions and access controls suited to complex organizational structures.

pros: Core software is free and open source with no vendor lock-in
Over 25 years of active development and a mature, stable codebase
Highly customizable workflows via Lifecycle builder and Scrips automation
Large ecosystem of 400+ free community extensions
Both self-hosted and managed Cloud RT deployment options available
cons: Self-hosting requires technical expertise to install, configure, and maintain
User interface is less modern-looking than many newer SaaS helpdesk tools
Best Practical Solutions is a small company (roughly 9-11 employees), which may limit support capacity
Managed Cloud RT and support pricing is not fully transparent on the primary site
Steeper learning curve than simpler out-of-the-box helpdesk tools

faqs.question: Is Request Tracker free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Request Tracker software is free and open source under the GNU General Public License for self-hosted use. Best Practical Solutions also offers paid hosted Cloud RT and support packages.

faqs.question: Who created Request Tracker?
faqs.answer: Request Tracker was first released in 1996 by Jesse Vincent, who later founded Best Practical Solutions LLC to develop and support it commercially.

faqs.question: Who maintains Request Tracker today?
faqs.answer: Best Practical Solutions LLC, a small company headquartered in Somerville, Massachusetts, maintains and supports Request Tracker.

faqs.question: What is RTIR?
faqs.answer: RT for Incident Response (RTIR) is a specialized variant of Request Tracker designed for security teams handling incident-response ticketing workflows.

faqs.question: Can Request Tracker be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, RT can be downloaded and self-hosted for free, or run through Best Practical's hosted Cloud RT service for organizations that prefer a managed deployment.

faqs.question: Does Request Tracker support automation?
faqs.answer: Yes, RT includes an automation engine called Scrips and Actions that triggers rules-based responses and notifications based on ticket events.

faqs.question: What languages does Request Tracker support?
faqs.answer: RT's interface supports more than 35 languages through community translations.

faqs.question: Does Request Tracker have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, RT includes a REST v2 API and supports Zapier integration for connecting to other tools.

enrichment.short_description: RERO ILS is a free, open-source integrated library system built on the CERN-developed Invenio framework, used mainly by public, school, and heritage libraries and library networks in Switzerland.
enrichment.pricing_model: open source (free), with implementation/hosting support available through RERO+
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source); implementation and hosting support terms available on request from RERO+
enrichment.seo_title: RERO ILS Review: Open-Source Library System Features and Overview
enrichment.seo_meta_description: An overview of RERO ILS, the open-source integrated library system developed in Switzerland on the Invenio framework, its features, and who uses it.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RERO ILS is a free, open-source integrated library system (ILS) developed beginning in 2017 by RERO, in collaboration with the Catholic University of Louvain (UCLouvain) in Belgium, as a next-generation replacement for RERO's legacy proprietary library software. The system is built on Invenio, a digital library framework originally developed at CERN, giving RERO ILS a research-grade technical foundation. RERO itself traces back to 1985, when it was created to manage the emerging IT resources of academic libraries in French-speaking Switzerland; on 29 April 2021, RERO was reconstituted as RERO+, a foundation of private law and public interest formally established by the Swiss cantons of Jura, Neuchatel, and Valais along with the city of Martigny, which now governs and develops RERO ILS. RERO ILS reached its first major stable release (v1.0.0) at the end of 2020, and its first real-world production library instance went live in June 2021, after several years of active development. The platform is designed to manage the full range of core library operations, including document acquisition, cataloguing, circulation and lending, and public-facing search and discovery interfaces, and it supports both independent libraries and consortial library networks operating under a shared system. RERO ILS is multilingual by design, with its interface available in French, German, Italian, and English, reflecting its primary user base of public, school, and heritage libraries and library networks across Switzerland, alongside international interest from other libraries seeking an open-source alternative to proprietary integrated library systems like Ex Libris Alma or SirsiDynix. Because RERO ILS is open source and collaboratively developed, there is no license fee to use or self-host the software; libraries and institutions that want a fully managed deployment, migration assistance, or ongoing technical support typically arrange terms directly with RERO+ or through community contribution channels, and organizations can explore the platform via a public test instance and contribute to its development through its GitHub repository and community chat channels. As a niche, sector-specific library system rather than a mainstream commercial SaaS product, RERO ILS is primarily relevant to library administrators, systems librarians, and consortium IT staff evaluating open-source alternatives to proprietary ILS platforms, rather than to general business software buyers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RERO ILS is an open-source integrated library system developed starting in 2017 by RERO, in collaboration with the Catholic University of Louvain in Belgium, intended to replace RERO's legacy proprietary library management software. It is built on Invenio, a digital library framework originally created at CERN, giving the system a research-grade, actively maintained technical foundation.

RERO, the organization behind the system, dates back to 1985 as a body managing IT resources for academic libraries in French-speaking Switzerland. On 29 April 2021 it was reconstituted as RERO+, a foundation of private law and public interest established by the Swiss cantons of Jura, Neuchatel, and Valais along with the city of Martigny, which now governs RERO ILS's ongoing development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: RERO ILS manages the full core workflow of a library, including document acquisitions, cataloguing, circulation and lending, and a public search and discovery interface, and it supports both standalone libraries and multi-library consortial networks operating on a shared instance.

The platform is multilingual by design, with interfaces available in French, German, Italian, and English, reflecting its primary deployment across public, school, and heritage libraries and library networks in Switzerland, while remaining open to adoption by libraries internationally.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RERO ILS is free and open source; there is no license fee to download, self-host, or modify the software, and its source code is maintained publicly for community contribution. A public test instance is available for institutions evaluating the system before committing to a migration.

Libraries seeking a managed deployment, data migration, or ongoing technical support typically work directly with RERO+ to arrange implementation and hosting terms, as pricing for managed services is not published and is handled on a case-by-case basis.

features.title: Cataloguing and acquisitions
features.description: Manages the full lifecycle of library materials from acquisition through cataloguing.

features.title: Circulation and lending
features.description: Handles checkouts, returns, holds, and other lending operations for library patrons.

features.title: Public discovery interface
features.description: Provides a public-facing search and browse interface for library users.

features.title: Multi-library network support
features.description: Supports both independent libraries and consortial library networks sharing a single system instance.

features.title: Built on Invenio (CERN)
features.description: Uses the Invenio digital library framework, originally developed at CERN, as its technical foundation.

features.title: Multilingual interface
features.description: Available in French, German, Italian, and English to serve Switzerland's multilingual library sector.

features.title: Open-source and community-driven
features.description: Source code is publicly available on GitHub, with community contributions welcomed via chat channels.

features.title: Public test instance
features.description: Institutions can evaluate the system on a public test deployment before migrating.

pros: Free and open source with no license fee required
Built on the CERN-developed Invenio framework, a mature research-grade technology base
Purpose-built for library-specific workflows like cataloguing and circulation
Multilingual interface suited to Switzerland's French, German, and Italian-speaking regions
Supports both single-library and multi-library consortium deployments
cons: Niche, sector-specific tool with limited relevance outside libraries and archives
Primarily adopted in Switzerland, with less documented international deployment
Self-hosting requires technical resources; managed hosting terms are not publicly published
Smaller ecosystem and community compared to major proprietary ILS vendors
Governed by a foundation model that may move more slowly than commercial software vendors

faqs.question: What is RERO ILS?
faqs.answer: RERO ILS is a free, open-source integrated library system for managing acquisitions, cataloguing, circulation, and public discovery, built on the CERN-developed Invenio framework.

faqs.question: Who developed RERO ILS?
faqs.answer: RERO ILS was developed starting in 2017 by RERO, in collaboration with the Catholic University of Louvain in Belgium, and is now governed by the RERO+ foundation.

faqs.question: Is RERO ILS free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, RERO ILS is open source and free to download and self-host; managed hosting or support arrangements are negotiated separately with RERO+.

faqs.question: Who uses RERO ILS?
faqs.answer: RERO ILS is used primarily by public, school, and heritage libraries and library networks in Switzerland, with interest from other libraries internationally.

faqs.question: What framework is RERO ILS built on?
faqs.answer: RERO ILS is built on Invenio, a digital library software framework originally developed at CERN.

faqs.question: When did RERO ILS launch in production?
faqs.answer: RERO ILS reached its first major release (v1.0.0) at the end of 2020, with the first real-world production library instance going live in June 2021.

faqs.question: What languages does RERO ILS support?
faqs.answer: RERO ILS's interface is available in French, German, Italian, and English.

faqs.question: What organization governs RERO ILS today?
faqs.answer: RERO+, a foundation of private law and public interest established in April 2021 by the Swiss cantons of Jura, Neuchatel, and Valais and the city of Martigny, governs RERO ILS.

enrichment.short_description: RescueTime is an automatic time-tracking and focus app that monitors app and website usage to help individuals and teams understand and improve their productivity habits.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Lite); paid Solo Focus plan from $7/month billed annually ($84/year)
enrichment.seo_title: RescueTime Review 2026: Pricing, Plans, and Focus Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how RescueTime's automatic time tracking, focus tools, and 2026 pricing plans compare for individuals and teams trying to improve productivity.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RescueTime is an automatic time-tracking and productivity application founded in 2006 by Tony Wright and Brian Fioca, headquartered in Seattle, Washington, and backed early on by Y Combinator, True Ventures, and Lowercase Capital. As of 2026, the company remains an independent, privately held business rather than being owned by a larger acquirer, and operates as a lean, distributed team of roughly 10 to 15 employees spread across the United States. RescueTime's core product runs in the background on a user's computer and mobile devices, automatically logging time spent in applications and websites without requiring manual timers, then categorizing that activity by productivity level to generate reports, trends, and a daily 'Productivity Pulse' score. Its differentiating features beyond passive tracking include FocusTime, which blocks distracting websites and apps during designated focus sessions; customizable goals and alerts that notify users when they exceed time budgets on distracting categories or fall short of focus targets; idle-time detection; and cross-device activity tracking spanning desktop and mobile. In 2025 and into 2026, RescueTime restructured its pricing around two feature tracks, Focus and Timesheets, offered in both individual (Solo) and team configurations. Solo Focus costs $7/month billed annually ($84/year) or $9/month billed monthly, and includes automatic tracking, distraction blocking, goals and alerts, idle-time detection, and personal reports. Solo+ adds Timesheets functionality — including a timeline view, client/project/task tracking, billable rates, and time rounding — for $12/month billed annually ($144/year) or $15/month billed monthly. Team Focus is priced per user at $10/month billed annually ($120/year per user) or $12/month billed monthly, and Team+ (Focus plus Timesheets) is $16/month billed annually ($192/year per user) or $18/month billed monthly, with both team tiers adding collaborative team-management and reporting tools. A free-forever Lite tier remains available with core automatic tracking and reporting but without the premium focus and timesheet features. All paid plans include a 14-day free trial, invoice billing is available for teams of 100 or more users, and the company charges no sales tax or VAT. RescueTime is widely used by remote workers, freelancers, and small teams who want passive, low-effort insight into how their time is actually spent across apps and websites, as well as by consultants and agencies that use the Timesheets tier to track billable client work without manually starting and stopping timers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RescueTime was founded in 2006 by Tony Wright and Brian Fioca and is headquartered in Seattle, Washington. The company built one of the earliest automatic time-tracking tools, designed to passively log how users spend time across applications and websites without requiring manual timers.

As of 2026, RescueTime remains an independently operated, privately held company rather than part of a larger parent company, running as a small, distributed team backed historically by Y Combinator, True Ventures, and Lowercase Capital.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: RescueTime's core function is automatic, passive tracking of time spent in desktop and mobile applications and websites, which it categorizes by productivity level to generate a daily Productivity Pulse score and detailed usage reports. Its FocusTime feature can actively block distracting sites and apps during scheduled focus sessions.

Additional capabilities include customizable goals and alerts, idle-time detection, and cross-device tracking. The Timesheets add-on, available on Solo+ and Team+ tiers, layers in client, project, and task-level time tracking with billable rates and time rounding for consultants and agencies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RescueTime offers a free Lite tier alongside four paid plans split across Solo and Team tiers and Focus versus Focus-plus-Timesheets feature sets. Solo Focus is $7/month billed annually ($84/year) or $9/month billed monthly; Solo+ (adding Timesheets) is $12/month billed annually ($144/year) or $15/month billed monthly.

Team Focus is $10/month per user billed annually ($120/year per user) or $12/month billed monthly; Team+ is $16/month per user billed annually ($192/year per user) or $18/month billed monthly. All paid plans include a 14-day free trial, and invoice billing is available for teams of 100 or more users.

features.title: Automatic time tracking
features.description: Passively logs time spent in applications and websites across desktop and mobile without manual timers.

features.description: Blocks distracting websites and apps during scheduled focus sessions to support deep work.

features.description: Set customizable time budgets and receive alerts when exceeding distracting-category usage or missing focus targets.

features.title: Idle time detection
features.description: Automatically detects and excludes idle periods from productivity reporting.

features.title: Cross-device tracking
features.description: Tracks activity across both desktop and mobile devices for a unified view of time use.

features.title: Timesheets add-on
features.description: Adds client, project, and task-level time tracking with billable rates and time rounding for consultants and agencies.

features.title: Team reporting and management
features.description: Team tiers add collaborative reporting and management tools for tracking productivity across a group.

pros: Fully automatic tracking requires no manual timers or check-ins
FocusTime distraction blocking helps enforce deep-work sessions
Free Lite tier available for basic automatic tracking and reporting
Timesheets add-on supports billable client work for freelancers and agencies
Remains independently operated with a long operating history since 2006
cons: Premium features (Focus and Timesheets) require a paid subscription
Team pricing is per-user, which can add up for larger teams
Small company size (roughly 10-15 employees) may limit support and feature velocity
Passive tracking requires installing and running background software on all devices
Some advanced project/billing features are gated behind the higher Solo+ or Team+ tiers

faqs.question: What does RescueTime do?
faqs.answer: RescueTime automatically tracks how much time you spend in different applications and websites across your devices, then categorizes that activity to help you understand and improve your productivity habits.

faqs.question: How much does RescueTime cost?
faqs.answer: RescueTime offers a free Lite tier, with paid plans starting at $7/month billed annually for Solo Focus, up to $18/month per user billed monthly for Team+ (Focus plus Timesheets).

faqs.question: Who founded RescueTime?
faqs.answer: RescueTime was founded in 2006 by Tony Wright and Brian Fioca and is headquartered in Seattle, Washington.

faqs.question: Is RescueTime still an independent company?
faqs.answer: Yes, as of 2026 RescueTime continues to operate as an independent, privately held company and has not been acquired by a larger parent company.

faqs.question: What is FocusTime in RescueTime?
faqs.answer: FocusTime is a feature that blocks distracting websites and applications during scheduled focus sessions to support uninterrupted work.

faqs.question: Does RescueTime track time automatically or manually?
faqs.answer: RescueTime tracks time automatically in the background across desktop and mobile devices, without requiring users to start or stop manual timers.

faqs.question: What is the Timesheets feature in RescueTime?
faqs.answer: Timesheets is an add-on available on the Solo+ and Team+ tiers that adds client, project, and task-level time tracking with billable rates and time rounding.

faqs.question: Does RescueTime offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, all paid RescueTime plans include a 14-day free trial.

enrichment.short_description: Resemble AI is a voice cloning and generative audio platform offering real-time text-to-speech, voice cloning, and AI-audio deepfake detection through a consumption-based API.
enrichment.starting_price: $0 to start (pay-as-you-go); usage from $0.0005 per synthesis second, voice clones from $2-$5/month each, team seats at $20/month per user
enrichment.seo_title: Resemble AI Review 2026: Voice Cloning Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: An overview of Resemble AI's voice cloning, real-time text-to-speech, and deepfake detection tools, plus its 2026 usage-based pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Resemble AI is a generative voice technology company founded in 2019 and headquartered in Santa Clara, California, with roughly 39 employees as of mid-2026 spread across North America, Asia, and Europe. The company has raised approximately $25 million in total funding across five rounds, including an $8 million Series A in July 2023 led by Javelin Venture Partners with participation from Comcast Ventures, bringing its total funding at that point to $12 million. Resemble AI's core product lets users create a synthetic voice clone from a sample of human speech and then generate new speech in that cloned voice from arbitrary text, and the company has expanded its product line significantly since 2024 to include real-time, low-latency streaming text-to-speech (around 75 milliseconds latency on its Chatterbox Turbo model), a deepfake-audio detection product called Detect, and an audio watermarking and provenance product called Verify. As of 2026, Resemble AI has moved to a fully consumption-based pricing model organized around two tiers: Flex, a pay-as-you-go plan where new accounts start with $0 required and credits that never expire, giving full API access from day one including voice cloning, Detect, and Verify; and Enterprise, a custom-priced tier with a deal floor generally in the high four-figures per month for committed spend. Under Flex, usage is billed granularly by feature: synthesis costs about $0.0005 per second of generated speech, each hosted voice clone costs roughly $2 to $5 per month to maintain, and additional team seats cost $20 per month per user; deepfake and content-intelligence detection is billed separately, with audio detection at about $0.04 per second, video detection at about $0.07 per second, image detection at about $0.04 per second, and audio/video/image 'intelligence' analysis at about $0.03 per second each, while identity search costs about $0.0005 per search and watermark encoding and decoding cost about $0.0005 and $0.0002 per second respectively. Enterprise customers receive volume discounts, elevated concurrency limits, enterprise-grade support SLAs, custom model training and tuning, SSO/SAML authentication, a dedicated support contact, and an on-premises deployment option. Resemble AI is used by media and entertainment companies for dubbing and localized voiceovers, by gaming studios for dynamic in-game character voices, by customer-experience and IVR teams building conversational voice agents, and increasingly by trust-and-safety and content-moderation teams using its Detect and Verify products to identify AI-generated or manipulated audio and video content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Resemble AI, founded in 2019 and headquartered in Santa Clara, California, builds generative voice technology centered on voice cloning: creating a synthetic voice from a sample of human speech that can then read arbitrary text aloud. The company has raised approximately $25 million in funding across five rounds, including an $8 million Series A in 2023 led by Javelin Venture Partners.

Since 2024, Resemble AI has expanded beyond core text-to-speech into real-time streaming voice synthesis, with its Chatterbox Turbo model achieving around 75ms latency, alongside two content-integrity products: Detect, for identifying AI-generated deepfake audio, video, and images, and Verify, for watermarking and provenance tracking of AI-generated content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform's core capability is voice cloning and text-to-speech synthesis from a short sample of human speech, paired with real-time streaming synthesis for low-latency conversational applications. Its Detect product analyzes audio, video, and image content to flag likely AI-generated or manipulated deepfakes, while Verify provides watermarking and identity-search tools for content provenance.

Resemble AI offers full API access alongside a web UI, letting developers integrate voice cloning and synthesis directly into applications such as IVR systems, games, dubbing pipelines, and content-moderation tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Resemble AI's 2026 pricing is fully consumption-based under its Flex plan, which requires no upfront payment, offers credits that never expire, and grants full API access from day one. Synthesis is billed at roughly $0.0005 per second of generated speech, each hosted voice clone costs about $2-$5 per month, and additional team seats are $20 per month per user.

Detection and verification features are billed separately and granularly: audio and image deepfake detection run about $0.04 per second, video detection about $0.07 per second, and 'intelligence' analysis about $0.03 per second across audio, video, and image; watermarking and identity search are billed at fractions of a cent per operation. Enterprise customers negotiate custom volume pricing, typically starting in the high four-figures per month for committed spend, in exchange for elevated limits, SLAs, and dedicated support.

features.title: Voice cloning
features.description: Creates a synthetic voice clone from a sample of human speech that can generate new speech from arbitrary text.

features.title: Real-time streaming TTS
features.description: Low-latency text-to-speech (around 75ms on Chatterbox Turbo) suited to live conversational voice applications.

features.title: Deepfake detection (Detect)
features.description: Analyzes audio, video, and image content to identify likely AI-generated or manipulated deepfakes.

features.title: Watermarking and verification (Verify)
features.description: Embeds and detects watermarks in AI-generated audio for provenance and authenticity tracking.

features.title: Identity search
features.description: Searches for a voice's identity match against known voice profiles for verification purposes.

features.title: Full API access
features.description: Complete programmatic access to voice cloning, synthesis, detection, and verification from the start of a Flex account.

features.title: Custom model training
features.description: Enterprise customers can access custom model training and tuning for specialized use cases.

features.title: On-premises deployment
features.description: Enterprise tier offers an on-premises deployment option for organizations with strict data requirements.

pros: Consumption-based pricing with no minimum spend to start on the Flex plan
Credits never expire under the Flex plan
Combines voice generation with deepfake detection and watermarking in one platform
Low-latency real-time streaming synthesis suitable for live conversational use
Enterprise tier offers on-premises deployment for sensitive data requirements
cons: Granular per-feature usage pricing can make total costs harder to predict upfront
Enterprise pricing is not public and starts at a high four-figure monthly commitment
Small team size (about 39 employees) relative to larger TTS competitors
Voice clone hosting fees ($2-$5/month each) add up for accounts managing many clones
Team seats are billed separately at $20/month per user on top of usage costs

faqs.question: What does Resemble AI do?
faqs.answer: Resemble AI is a generative voice platform that clones voices from speech samples and generates new synthetic speech, alongside deepfake detection and content watermarking tools.

faqs.question: How does Resemble AI pricing work?
faqs.answer: Resemble AI uses a consumption-based Flex plan with no upfront cost, billing about $0.0005 per second of synthesized speech, $2-$5/month per hosted voice clone, and $20/month per team seat, plus separate per-second rates for deepfake detection.

faqs.question: When was Resemble AI founded?
faqs.answer: Resemble AI was founded in 2019 and is headquartered in Santa Clara, California.

faqs.question: What is Resemble AI's Detect product?
faqs.answer: Detect is Resemble AI's deepfake detection product that analyzes audio, video, and image content to identify likely AI-generated or manipulated media.

faqs.question: What is Resemble AI's Verify product?
faqs.answer: Verify is Resemble AI's watermarking and content-provenance product, used to embed and detect watermarks in AI-generated audio.

faqs.question: Does Resemble AI support real-time voice synthesis?
faqs.answer: Yes, Resemble AI offers real-time streaming text-to-speech with latency around 75 milliseconds on its Chatterbox Turbo model.

faqs.question: How much funding has Resemble AI raised?
faqs.answer: Resemble AI has raised approximately $25 million in total funding across five rounds, including an $8 million Series A in 2023.

faqs.question: Does Resemble AI offer an Enterprise plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Resemble AI offers a custom-priced Enterprise plan with volume discounts, elevated concurrency limits, SLAs, custom model training, SSO/SAML, and on-premises deployment.

use_cases.title: Media dubbing and localization
use_cases.description: Media and entertainment companies use Resemble AI to generate localized voiceovers and dubbing in cloned voices across languages.

use_cases.title: Conversational voice agents
use_cases.description: Customer experience teams use Resemble AI's real-time streaming synthesis to power low-latency conversational IVR and voice agents.
use_cases.audience: Customer experience and IVR engineering teams

use_cases.title: Deepfake and content moderation
use_cases.description: Trust-and-safety teams use Resemble AI's Detect and Verify products to identify AI-generated or manipulated audio and video content.

features: Full API access from day one

pricing_plans.price: Custom (high four-figures monthly commitment and up)
